content.problemStatement:
Read problem statements in Vietnamese,Akash has just learned the maximum subarray sum problem. And while thinking about the solution, he came up with a new problem, the maximum frequent subarray sum problem.

In this problem, you will be given an array AAA of NNN integers. You have to choose a non-empty subarray with the maximum possible score. The score of a subarray is calculated as
score(l,r)=(Al+⋯+Ar)⋅(occurrences)\mathrm{score}(l, r) = (A_l+\cdots+A_r) \cdot (\mathrm{occurrences})score(l,r)=(Al​+⋯+Ar​)⋅(occurrences)
Here, occurrences\mathrm{occurrences}occurrences is the number of occurrences of that subarray in AAA.

Now Akash can't solve this problem, so please help him solve it.

###Input


The first line contains an integer TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer nnn, the size of the array.
The second line contains nnn integers A1,…,ANA_1,\ldots, A_NA1​,…,AN​.
###Output
For each test case, output the maximum possible score in a new line.

###Constraints


1≤T≤2⋅1051 \leq T \leq 2 \cdot 10^51≤T≤2⋅105
1≤n≤1051 \leq n \leq 10^51≤n≤105
−107≤Ai≤107-10^7 \leq A_i \leq 10^7−107≤Ai​≤107
The sum of nnn over all test cases ≤3⋅105\leq 3 \cdot 10^5 ≤3⋅105
###Subtasks


**Subtask 1 (10 points):** 1≤n≤1001 \leq n \leq 100 1≤n≤100, the sum of nnn over all test cases ≤300\leq 300≤300
**Subtask 2 (15 points):** 1≤n≤10001 \leq n \leq 1000 1≤n≤1000, the sum of nnn over all test cases ≤3000\leq 3000≤3000
**Subtask 3 (75 points):** original constraints
###Sample Input

`2
6
10 8 -20 5 5 5 
10
-5 1 7 -1 2 -4 10 0 -11 3
`###Sample Output

`20
15
`###Explanation
In the first test case, the maximum score is attained by subarray [5,5][5, 5][5,5], its score is (5+5)⋅2=20(5+5) \cdot 2 = 20(5+5)⋅2=20 since it occurs twice in AAA.

In the second test case, the maximum score is attained by both subarrays [1,7,−1,2,−4,10][1, 7, -1, 2, -4, 10][1,7,−1,2,−4,10] and [1,7,−1,2,−4,10,0][1, 7, -1, 2, -4, 10, 0][1,7,−1,2,−4,10,0]. Both have sum 151515 and occur once, so their scores are 15⋅1=1515 \cdot 1 = 15 15⋅1=15.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. For each test case, the first line contains an integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 10^5), the size of the array. The second line contains n space‑separated integers A1 … An (|Ai| ≤ 10^7).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the maximum possible score of a non‑empty subarray, defined as (sum of the subarray) × (number of its occurrences in the whole array). Each answer is printed on its own line.

solution.approach: For a subarray that appears only once the score equals its sum, therefore the classic maximum subarray sum (Kadane) is a candidate. For subarrays that appear at least twice we need the number of occurrences. Treat the array as a string of integers and build a suffix array (SA) in O(n log n). The LCP array gives the length of the longest common prefix of two consecutive suffixes. Any substring that occurs k≥2 times corresponds to a contiguous segment of the SA where the minimum LCP in that segment is at least the length of the substring. By scanning the LCP array with a monotone stack (the same technique as the largest rectangle in a histogram) we enumerate every distinct substring that appears at least twice: when a height h (an LCP value) is popped we know the maximal segment of suffixes where all LCPs are ≥ h. The number of suffixes in that segment is occ = width + 1 and the substring length is h. All those suffixes share the same first h elements, therefore the sum of the substring is the same for any of them. We obtain the sum by taking the minimum starting index among the suffixes in the segment (any index works) and using a prefix‑sum array. To retrieve the minimum start index quickly we build a Sparse Table over the SA array (RMQ for minimum). For each popped height we compute score = sum * occ and keep the maximum. Finally the answer is max( KadaneScore , bestScoreFromLCP ). The whole algorithm runs in O(n log n) time per test case (dominated by suffix‑array construction) and O(n) additional memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n log n) per test case (suffix array construction) + O(n) for LCP processing and RMQ queries.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) for SA, rank arrays, LCP, prefix sums and the sparse table.
